coredata.dc:title: Voltammetric analysis of As3+ and sulfamethoxazole on an electrode modified with a composite of carbon dots and graphitic carbon nitride
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Moundzounga, Theo H.G.
Peleyeju, Moses G.
Orimolade, Benjamin O.
Olatunde, Abimbola M.
Maboya, Winny K.
Akpotu, Samson O.
Chili, Muntuwenkosi M.
Klink, Michael J.
coredata.dc:description: The need to monitor priority pollutants in the aqueous environments cannot be overemphasised. Herein, we employed a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with a nanocomposite of carbondots (CDs) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) for the determination of arsenic(III) and sulfamethoxazole (SMX) in aqueous media. The composite material, synthesised by microwave technique, was characterised by transmission electron microscopy and X-ray diffraction. The electrochemical behaviours of the modified glassy electrode were also investigated using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulsed voltammetry. The findings indicated that the constructed g-C3N4/CDs/GCE electrode markedly improved the oxidation peak currents of both analytes, suggesting enhanced sensitivities. Notably, the working ranges and detection limits obtained for arsenic (III) were 2 – 10 and 1.64 μM and 0.3 – 0.1 and 0.10 μM for SMX, respectively. Though, copper, cadmium, lead and zinc were observed to interfere with the determination of As3+, but EDTA was used to mask these interfering effects of the cations. The modified electrode (g- C3N4/CDs/GCE) was used to quantify arsenic and SMX in spiked aqueous solution by standard addition and percentage recoveries of 98–100% and 80–105% were recorded for As3+ and SMX respectively. Thus, the findings of this study established that the investigated nanocomposite material represents an easy and sensitive platform for the determination of As3+ and SMX in the aqueous environments to curb environmental pollution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride
Carbon dots
Heavy metals
Pharmaceuticals
Electrochemical sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of g-C3N4, carbon dots (CDs) and g-C3N4/CDs
Electrochemical analysis of arsenic (III) and Sulfamethoxazole in aqueous solution
Characterization of the CDs, g-C3N4, and g-C3N4/CDs nanomaterials
Structural and morphological characterization of CDs, g-C3N4, and g-C3N4/CDs
Effect of the amount of g-C3N4/CDs dispersion
Electrochemical behaviours of SMX and As (III) at different electrodes
Voltammetric detection of SMX and As(III)
Investigations on the reproducibility and stability of the modified electrodes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of a) CDs and b) g-C3N4/CDs.
XRD patterns of CDs, g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CDs.
CV of GCE, CDs/GCE, g-C3N4/GCE and g-C3N4/CDs/GCE in 5 mM [Ru (NH3)6]2+/3+ containing 0.1 M KCl at scan rate of 50 mV/s.
2 DPV plots of a) 2 μM As (III), b) 0.5 μM SMX at g-C3N4/CDs/GCE in 0.1 M PBS at different pH values.
a) The effect of the amount of g-C3N4/CDs modifier on the oxidation current of 0.9 μM SMX, and b) The effect of the amount of g-C3N4/CDs modifier on the oxidation current of 8 μM As (III).
DPV plots of GCE, g-C3N4/GCE, CDS/GCE and g-C3N4/CDs/CGE a) in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 5) containing 0.7 μM of SMX and b) 0.1 M PBS (pH = 6) containing 6 μM of As (III).

a) DPV plots of various concentrations of As (III) (2–10 μM) at g-C3N4/CDs/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 6), b) The linear plot oxidation peak current vs concentration of As (III), c) DPV plots of various concentrations of SMX (0.3–1.1 μM) at g-C3N4/CDs/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 5), d) The linear plot of oxidation peak current vs concentration of SMX.

Reproducibility of g-C3N4/CDs/CGE electrode with 5 different DPV measurements for a) 2 μM As (III) in 0.01 M PBS at pH 6 and b) 1.1 μM As SMX in 0.01 M PBS at pH 5. Stability of g-C3N4/CDs/CGE electrode with repetitive DPV measurements over two weeks for c) 4 μM As (III) in 0.01 M PBS at pH 6 and d) 0.5 μM in 0.01 M PBS at pH 5.
DPV plots of 2 μM As (III) a) with 2 μM of different interfering ions b) with different interfering ions + 2 mM EDTA.
Comparison of the performance of different electrochemical sensors for As (III) detection.
Comparison of different electrodes for the analysis of SMX in aqueous media.
Application of g-C3N4/CDs/CGE sensor for the determination of As (III) in effluent and tap water samples.
Application of g-C3N4/CDs/CGE sensor for the detection of SMX in effluent and tap water samples.

head.ce:title: Voltammetric analysis of As3+ and sulfamethoxazole on an electrode modified with a composite of carbon dots and graphitic carbon nitride

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Natural Sciences, Vaal University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The need to monitor priority pollutants in the aqueous environments cannot be overemphasised. Herein, we employed a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with a nanocomposite of carbondots (CDs) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) for the determination of arsenic(III) and sulfamethoxazole (SMX) in aqueous media. The composite material, synthesised by microwave technique, was characterised by transmission electron microscopy and X-ray diffraction. The electrochemical behaviours of the modified glassy electrode were also investigated using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulsed voltammetry. The findings indicated that the constructed g-C3N4/CDs/GCE electrode markedly improved the oxidation peak currents of both analytes, suggesting enhanced sensitivities. Notably, the working ranges and detection limits obtained for arsenic (III) were 2 – 10 and 1.64 μM and 0.3 – 0.1 and 0.10 μM for SMX, respectively. Though, copper, cadmium, lead and zinc were observed to interfere with the determination of As3+, but EDTA was used to mask these interfering effects of the cations. The modified electrode (g- C3N4/CDs/GCE) was used to quantify arsenic and SMX in spiked aqueous solution by standard addition and percentage recoveries of 98–100% and 80–105% were recorded for As3+ and SMX respectively. Thus, the findings of this study established that the investigated nanocomposite material represents an easy and sensitive platform for the determination of As3+ and SMX in the aqueous environments to curb environmental pollution.

ce:section.ce:para: The release of untreated wastewater into the environment leads to the contamination of available water sources; and scarcity of quality drinking water creates a significant health challenge to both humans and animals. A typical wastewater may contain impurities such as harmful bacteria, organic pollutants and heavy metals, and so on [1,2]. If the amounts of contaminants in water exceed the established safe limits, they can have harmful effects on humans and the environment [3]. Heavy metal ions have the tendency to accumulate in the living organisms and can form strong bond with protein molecules in living cells, leading to various diseases [4]. As a result of their wide distribution, high toxicity and persistence, heavy metals are dangerous pollutants [5]. They get into water sources through several channels such as metallurgical processes, mining activities, chemical industries, amongst others [6,7]. In particular, the presence of arsenic in aqueous environments is of great concern for the global community due to its toxicity and severe impact on human health [3,8]. Hence, the World Health Organization (WHO) provided a guideline on the maximum allowable arsenic concentration in potable water as 10 μg/L (10 ppb) [9,10]. Elevated levels or prolonged exposure to arsenic can result in vitamin A deficiency, which is linked to night blindness, heart disease, hypertension, as well as other conditions such as cerebrovascular disease, drowsiness, and cancer. [11]. Therefore, monitoring drinking water for the presence of arsenic has become a focus in the last decade [12].

Organic pollutants including pharmaceuticals are another class of water pollutants that can pose risks to the health of humans and animals [13]. The main categories of pharmaceuticals that have been identified in wastewater and environmental samples include antibiotics, analgesics, antipyretics, antimicrobials, anti-inflammatories, and hormones [14]. The presence of antibiotics in the environment and water sources can be attributed to the uncontrolled discharge of unused antibiotics by laboratories, factories, medical practices and households. Sulfamethoxazole (SMX), a member of the sulfonamide antibiotic family, has been found in the environment [15,16]. On the basis of the no observed effect concentration (NOEC) of the most sensitive organisms, the predicted no effect concentration (PNEC) of 520 ng/L of SMX in effluent is set as a limit [17]. Continuous consumption of sulfamethoxazole can lead to shortness of breath, headache and rapid heart rate [18]. In addition, consistent exposure of microbial organisms to SMX in the environment can lead to the proliferation of resistant strains of the organisms and this may give rise to general antibiotic resistance [19]. Therefore, it is important to monitor the SMX concentrations in water using an analytical device that is sensitive, stable and reliable [20].

The determination of pharmaceuticals in aqueous media have been achieved using coupled chromatographic and spectroscopic techniques. Also, heavy metals have been quantified in water using techniques such as atomic absorbance spectroscopy (AAS), atomic emission spectroscopy (AES) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [2,21,22]. Although most of these techniques can accurately detect very low amounts of the analytes, they however have some limitations including complex expensive hardware, sample handling, long analysis time and high costs [23]. To overcome these limitations, electrochemical techniques have been advanced [24]. These methods can be very sensitive and selective, and are comparably cheap. Electrochemical analyses are also simple to use and produce results in a relatively short time [25]. Although bare electrode (working electrode) can be utilised for electrochemical detection, their use is impeded by several drawbacks including sluggish kinetics and poor sensitivity and reproducibility. However, the performance of a bare electrode can be significantly enhanced by modifying its surface with an electron transfer mediator. An electron mediator assists in speeding up the charge transfer between the sensor and the target analyte. Several materials such as metal nanoparticles, metals oxides, metal sulfides, surfactants, conducting polymers, and carbon materials have been utilized to modify electrode surfaces [26].

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a relatively new nitrogen-doped, carbon-based material. It is made up of carbon, nitrogen and trace amount of hydrogen connected in a structure which features tri-s-triazine-based patterns [27,28]. g-C3N4 has become a promising material in sensing (electrochemical detection and bio sensing) due to its impressive catalytic activity and abundant nitrogen active sites that serve as active sites for electron conductivity [29,30]. However, the limited conductivity of g-C3N4 is a drawback for its application in electrochemical sensing for trace detection of analytes. Therefore, to improve the conductivity and sensitivity of g-C3N4, composites made of g-C3N4 and other more conducting materials have been studied for electrochemical applications by several researchers [31–33].

Carbon nanomaterials have been identified as good electrode materials for sensing applications because of their impressive properties. For example, functionalized multiwalled carbon naontubes have been reported for the detection of numerous kinds of organic pollutants [34–36]. Another fascinating carbon material for electrode modification is the carbon quantum dots. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are non-toxic nanomaterials with a particle size of less than 10 nm. They have been used in many fields for analytical applications [37]. CDs have attracted increasing attention from researchers because of their characteristics, including a large surface area and high catalytic efficiency. These attributes indicate that CDs could be an excellent electrode material for many electrochemical applications [38]. In recent studies, bare electrodes have been modified with CDs to accelerate inter-facial kinetics and thus the redox reaction processes [39]. Hence, it has a great potential to amplify the electrochemical properties of g-C3N4. Previously, the photocatalytic properties of gC3N4 has been improved with CDs [40,41]. However, the composite has not been used for the development of a sensor for monitoring SMX. An electrochemical sensor made of the nanocomposite will exhibit efficient electron transfer and high electroactive surface area. Therefore, the focus of this study was to prepare an electrode with a nanocomposite of graphitic carbon nitride and carbon dots for the determination of arsenic (III) and sulfamethoxazole in aqueous solutions. Firstly, the materials were synthesized and characterized using microscopic and electrochemical techniques. The nanocomposite was then used to modify glassy carbon electrodes (GCE) and optimized for the determination of the analytes in real water samples.

Disodium hydrogen phosphate (99.5%) and Monosodium hydrogen phosphate (99%) and cadmium nitrate trihydrate (99%) were procured from Merck (South Africa). Potassium ferrocyanide (99%), lead nitrate (99.5%) and zinc acetate (99.5%) were all purchased from Labochem (South Africa). Cupric chloride dehydrate (98%), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (99%), was purchased from ACE Chemicals (South Africa). Potassium chloride (99.5%) was purchased from Glassworld (South Africa). Dimethylformamide (99%) was purchased from Fisher Scientific (South Africa). Thiourea (99%), sodium meta arsenite (90%) and sulfamethoxazole (98%) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (South Africa). All chemicals were used as supplied. For electrochemical measurements, 0.1 M PBS (phosphate buffer solution) was used as a supporting electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of g-C3N4, carbon dots (CDs) and g-C3N4/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The pristine and composite materials were prepared as previously reported [27]. Briefly, a mixture comprising about 10 g of thiourea and 5 mL of distilled water was transferred into an alumina crucible and heated in a microwave oven at 600 W for 15 min. Thereafter, the material was collected and heated in air atmosphere using a tube furnace at a heating rate of 5 °C per min. The tube furnace was kept at 550 °C for 60 min. A yellow powder was obtained after the heat treatment.

To prepare carbon dots, 10 g of pine cone was dispersed in 50 mL ultrapure water and the mixture was placed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Subsequently, the autoclave was kept in an air oven maintained at 180 °C for 24 h. After filtration, the residue obtained was dried in an oven.

The composite of g-C3N4 and CDs was prepared by mixing 10 g of thiourea with 5 mL of the dispersion of CDs. An alumina crucible containing the mixture was then heated in a microwave oven set at 600 W for 15 min. A solid material was then collected and heated in a tube furnace to 550 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min. It was kept in the tube at 550 °C for 1 h.

Prior to modification, bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was successively cleaned by polishing with alumina slurries and then sonicated in ethyl alcohol and distilled water for a minute. Modified GCE was prepared by dropping a dimethyl formamide (DMF) dispersion of the materials on the surface of the cleaned electrode. The electrode was then left to dry under ambient condition. The bare and modified glassy carbon electrodes are used as working electrodes (WE).

The microscopic characterisation of the materials was carried out on a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL JEM-2100) operated at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction patterns were obtained on a Shimadzu-XRD 700 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.154056 Å), at a scan rate of 1°/min and at a current and a voltage of 30 mA and 40 kV respectively. Electrochemical measurements were run on a Bio-Logic SP-240 potentiostat, using Ag/AgCl (3.0 M KCl) and a platinum wire as reference electrode (RE) and counter electrode (CE) respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis of arsenic (III) and Sulfamethoxazole in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of the analytes were prepared. For As (III) sensing, the modified electrodes (WE), CE and RE were immersed in an electrochemical cell containing an electrolyte of the metal ion, and the electrodes were connected to the potentiostat. Differential pulse voltammograms (DPV) were taken at optimised parameters and the peak currents were noted. Sulfamethoxazole was analysed following similar procedure except that the pH of the buffer electrolyte was adjusted to about 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CDs, g-C3N4, and g-C3N4/CDs nanomaterials
Structural and morphological characterization of CDs, g-C3N4, and g-C3N4/CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images showing the morphologies of the prepared CDs and g-C3N4/CDs are presented in Fig. 1(a) and 1(b), respectively. The CDs can be clearly seen as well-dispersed particles. In Fig. 1(b), the morphology of g-C3N4 are seen as nanosheets containing many pores. However, the CDs cannot be easily noticed in the TEM of g-C3N4/CDs which could be attributed to the relatively large sizes of g-C3N4 nanosheets. Nevertheless, the structural characterisation of the materials through XRD further confirmed the successful synthesis of the materials. As depicted in Fig. 2, the g-C3N4 displayed a characteristic peak at 27.81° which relates to the distinct peak characteristic of graphitic carbon materials (JCPDS card no. 066–0813). This peak, though with a reduced intensity, was also noted in the XRD pattern of g-C3N4/CDs which revealed that the g-C3N4 was still present in the composite. The reduction in the intensity of the peak in the composite could be due to the incorporation of CDs. The broad peak of CDs was not observed in the diffraction pattern of the composite probably due to the low amount of CDs in the composite material.

Bare (GCE), CDs/GCE, g-C3N4/CDs, and g-C3N4/CDs/GCE sensors were electrochemically characterized with CV using [Ru(NH3)6]2+/3+ as redox probe. Electrochemical analyses were performed to compare the peak currents of the different sensors. As depicted in Fig. 3, the presence of CDs and g-C3N4 played a role in enhancing the interfacial electron transfer of the ruthenium probe. The maximum peak current was obtained at the g-C3N4/CDs/GCE electrode (50μA) compared to 47, 41 and 38 μA for CDs/GCE, g-C3N4/GCE and GCE electrodes respectively, suggesting a good electronic conductivity of g-C3N4/CDs/GCE electrode. The enhancement in peak current can be attributed to the better electroactive surface area of g-C3N4/CDs/GCE as compared to the other electrodes [42].

The selection of optimum pH for As (III) and sulfamethoxazole detection was studied with DPV. Fig. 4(a) shows the effect of pH on As (III) oxidation and it can be seen that both pH 1 and pH 3 displayed wider and poorly defined peaks despite having lower oxidation potentials (0.48 and 0.54 V, respectively) compared to that of pH 6 (0.72 V). The maximum peak current (13.8 μA) was obtained at pH 6, thus, the electroanalysis of As (III) was carried out at this pH. It is known that the ionic state of metals can be affected by the pH of their solutions. In Fig. 4(b), the effect of different pH values (from 4 to 7) on the oxidation peak current of sulfamethoxazole was investigated. The peak currents obtained at acidic pH were significantly higher that recorded at neutral pH. The optimum pH was found to be 5, because a well-defined and maximum oxidation peak current was observed at this pH. It appears that the oxidation of SMX is kinetically favoured with increasing [H+] in the electrolyte, which implies that the protonated SMX undergoes oxidation faster than the neutral SMX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the amount of g-C3N4/CDs dispersion
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the amount of the modifier dispersion on the oxidation peaks of the analytes were examined. As depicted in Fig. 5(a), when the amount of the g-C3N4/CDs increased from 6 μL to 12 μL, the oxidation peak current also progressively improved (from 18.28 to 19.89 μA) for SMX. However, when the dispersion volume exceeded 12 μL, the oxidation peak current began to diminish. Thus, the amount of g-C3N4/CDs dispersion was fixed at 12 μL. The same trend was observed for the effect of the amount of g-C3N4/CDs dispersion on the oxidation of As (III) in (Fig. 5(b)). The peak current increased (from 15.6 to 20.1 μA) when the amount of g-C3N4/CDs increased (from 6 to 10 μL). A decrease of peak current was also observed after a load amount of 10 μL. Thus, the amount of g-C3N4/CDs dispersion was fixed at 10 µL. The peak current enhancement can indicate that the number of catalytic sites and specific surface areas increased with an increase in the amount of g-C3N4/CDs dispersion on the surface of the glassy carbon electrode. It can be reasonably inferred that the specific surface area and the number of catalytic sites increased with increasing g-C3N4/CDs. The decrease in currents observed when the optimum amount of the modifier is used may indicate that excess material hinders electron transfer between the analytes and the electrode [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviours of SMX and As (III) at different electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of SMX and As (III) was performed first at four different electrodes (GCE, g- C3N4/GCE, CDs/GCE and g-C3N4/CDs/GCE) ( Fig. 6). As illustrated in Fig. 6(b), the GCE electrode was not sensitive enough to detect As (III) ions as it registered the weakest peak intensity with a higher oxidation potential (1.67 μA, 0.87 V) compared to the g-C3N4/CDs/GCE electrode which displayed higher sensitivity with a higher peak intensity (26.45 μA). Similarly, as shown in Fig. 6(a), the bare electrode sensitivity to SMX oxidation was poor, for non-important peak was observed. A higher oxidation peak for SMX (17.8 μA, 0.65 V) was observed at g-C3N4/CDs/GCE electrode compared to the other electrodes (g-C3N4/GCE and CDs/GCE). SMX can be electrochemically active due to the presence of some amino group on its surface [37]. The sensitivity of the sensor was found to be significantly enhanced when modified with g-C3N4/CDs as evident in the peak current enhancement. The remarkable peak currents improvement proved the electrocatalytic activity of g-C3N4/GCE electrode in the oxidation of sulfamethoxazole and As (III). This may be due to the increase in the electro-active surface area of the bare electrode (GCE) when modified with g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite. Hence, the differential pulse voltammograms of different concentrations of As (III) and SMX were obtained using g-C3N4/CDs/GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Voltammetric detection of SMX and As(III)
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance of g-C3N4/CDs/CGE electrode for the analysis of As (III) and SMX in 0.1 mol/L PBS solution was further evaluated using optimum experimental parameters with different concentrations As (III) and SMX. The DPV plots of different As (III) concentrations were recorded, the oxidation peak currents varied linearly with concentration of the metal ion as shown in Fig. 7(a). The calibration graph is shown in Fig. 7(b). The linear regression equation for the measurement of As (III) ions was y = 2.2954x − 1.3313, with R2=0.978. The limit of detection, LOD, was determined using the relation 3σ/m (where σ and m are the standard deviation of the blank and the slope of the calibration plot, respectively). The LOD was calculated to be 1.64 μM or 123 ppb (123 µg/L). The peak current SMX also increased linearly with the concentrations, while the peak potential remains almost unchanged (Fig. 7(c)). A linear response segment of SMX was obtained (Fig. 7(d)). The linear regression equation, which was expressed as Ipa (μA) = 15.771 C SMX (μmol/L) + 2.6041 (R
2 = 0.9906). The LOD was determined to be 0.10 μM. These results indicate that g-C3N4/CDs/CGE could be applied for the detection and quantification of SMX. The low LOD obtained for As (III) and SMX can be attributed to the enhancement of the signals of the analytes at the electrode owing to the presence of CDs and g-C3N4 nanoparticles modifiers.

The performance of the various prepared electrodes towards arsenic (III) and SMX electro- oxidation are compared. As presented in Table 1, the sensor based on the g-C3N4/CDs/CGE is not the most sensitive electrode for As (III) but it exhibits considerably high performance. The linear range and LOD values for SMX detection were also compared with a set of previous works where the analyte was sensed on different electroactive surfaces. It can be observed in Table 2 that g-C3N4/CDs/CGE modified electrode exhibits high sensitivity and was among the electrodes displaying lower LOD (0.10 μM). The good electrode performance could result from the high surface area of the g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite, leading to an increase of electrochemical active sites which enhanced its sensitivity toward SMX analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigations on the reproducibility and stability of the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility of g-C3N4/CDs/GCE electrode was estimated by doing 5 different DPV analysis of 2 μM of As (III) ( Fig. 8(a)) and 0.5 μM of SMX (Fig. 8(b)). The RSD was calculated to be 7.5% and 6.1% for As (III) and SMX respectively, implying good reproducibility of the fabricated sensing platform.

To measure the stability of the g-C3N4/CDs/GCE sensor, the electrode was kept at 4 °C for 15 days and the current response of 4 µM of As (III) (Fig. 8(c)) and 1.1 µM of SMX (Fig. 8(d)) were measured each 3 days. Notably, the stability of the electrode toward arsenic and sulfamethoxazole determination showed a decrease in peak intensity over the 15 days, which could be explained by the leaching of the nanocomposite material into the electrolyte or by the passivation of the surface after multiple runs [53].

A drawback of arsenic sensing using DPV is that its current signal can be interfered with by other metal ions which oxidise at potentials very close to that of As. This often results in a change in the current intensity of arsenic ions during measurement [7]. Of the many cations that have been investigated, copper presents the most significant interference [54]. Copper has been reported to form intermetallic bond with arsenic in solution, the presence of the resulting compound causes the current response of arsenic ions to become elevated [55]. Interference from various metal ions during arsenic sensing can be overcome by masking with EDTA. EDTA forms chelating complexes with the interfering ions, thus making them bulky enough and thus minimise/eliminate their interfering effect.

In the present study, we examined the interfering effects of some metal ions to assess the selectivity of the proposed sensor for arsenic detection. The g-C3N4/CDs/CGE was observed to be prone to interference from Pb2+, Cu2+, Cd2+ and Zn2+ with an increase in peak current of 22%, 45%, 47% and 53%, respectively ( Fig. 9(a)). This increase in peak intensity can be attributed to the proximity of the oxidation potentials of those metal ions with that of arsenic; and also the formation of intermetallic bonding between interfering ions and arsenic as depicted in the following equations:

When EDTA was added, the peak intensity for all interfering ions decreased (27%, 29%, 30%, and 76% decrease) for Pb2+, Zn2+, Cd2+ and Cu2+, respectively (Fig. 9(b)). This may be due to the formation of chelating complexes (see equations below) between EDTA and the interfering ions, making them bulky enough, thus allowing only arsenic detection. EDTA prevented inter-metallic bonding between the interfering ions and arsenic, thus increased the selectivity of the fabricated sensor;
(3.9) Cu2+ (aq) + (EDTA)2- (aq) → Cu(EDTA)
2-
(aq) + 2H+ (aq)
(3.10) Zn2+ (aq) + (EDTA)2- (aq) → Zn(EDTA)
2-
(aq) + 2H+ (aq)
(3.11) Cd2+ (aq) + (EDTA)2- (aq) → Cd(EDTA)
2- (aq) + 2H+ (aq)
(3.12) Pb2+ (aq) + (EDTA)2- (aq) → Pb(EDTA)
2- (aq) + 2H+ (aq)

Prior to analyses, the water samples (tap and effluent water) were prepared with PBS such that the final solution has 0.1 M PBS at pH 7.6. The recoveries from the water samples were measured by spiking water samples. Known concentrations of Arsenic(III) (3.5 and 7 μM) and sulfamethoxazole (0.4, 0.6 and 0.8 μM) were added to the effluent and tap water samples and detected by DPV method. The results are shown in Table 3 and Table 4 for arsenic and sulfamethoxazole respectively. The percentages of recovery of arsenic were between 94 and 105% and for sulfamethoxazole between 88 and 100%, showing that the proposed sensor is suitable for analytical applications. The RSD value below 5 and 6% for arsenic and sulfamethoxazole respectively, suggests that the precision of the proposed is reliable and it could be used for the detection of arsenic and sulfamethoxazole in the real environment samples.

In this work, a nanocomposite comprising carbon dots and g-C3N4 was successfully prepared, characterised and used to modify glassy carbon electrodes for the electroanalysis of As (III) and SMX in water. As envisaged, the combined beneficial effects of g-C3N4 and CDs resulted in the nano-composite displaying high electron transfer, greater electroactive surface area and hence, improved sensitivity toward the analytes. The sensor was used under optimal conditions for the quantitative determination of As (III) and SMX. Low detection limits for the two analytes were obtained (1.64 and 0.10 μM for As (III) and SMX, respectively). The nanocomposite exhibited notable current enhancement and good sensitivity in the determination of As and SMX, however, it exhibited sigificant disintegration over time. The sensor was used successfully for the practical analysis of As (III) and SMX in real water samples. The recovery percentages for the detection of known analyte concentrations in the real water samples were within the acceptable range (80- 120%), confirming the reliability and analytical performance of the sensor. This work provided an easy and sensitive tool for the comprehensive monitoring of arsenic and SMX in water towards managing environmental pollution

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Vaal University of Technology is gratefully acknowledged for funding this work.
